coredata.dc:title: Synergistic enhancement in PEC hydrogen production using novel GQDs and CuO modified TiO2 based heterostructure photocatalyst

coredata.dc:description: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting technique is one of the most promising, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly techniques for solar H2 production. However, the widely accepted TiO2 semiconductor photocatalyst for a PEC system portrays less visible light absorption due to its wide bandgap and rapid recombination of e−/h+ pairs that ultimately lead to its low hydrogen production efficiency. Herein this work, heterostructure graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and cupric oxide (CuO) modified TiO2 based photocatalyst were prepared to elucidate the optoelectronic and charge transfer properties of the TiO2 based photocatalysts to enhance their photocatalytic performances. The GQD and CuO modified TiO2 based photocatalysts were synthesized via hydrothermal synthesis technique at calcination temperature of 450 °C for calcination durations up to 3 h. The effect of the dopants was investigated on various physiochemical properties including structural, morphological, chemical, elemental, optoelectronic and as well as, photoelectrochemical properties. The developed CuO/GQD@TiO2 heterostructure photocatalyst presented the lowest energy bandgap (2.16 eV), enhanced visible light absorption (up to ∼745 nm), and reduced recombination of the charge carriers. The optimized tri-layered novel CuO/GQD@TiO2 photocatalyst demonstrated maximum H2 production up to 34,466 μmol g−1h−1 with photoconversion efficiency ∼9.01 %. Overall, the presented mechanistic insight and targeted strategy of incorporating CuO and GQD into TiO2 photocatalyst provides a fresh perspective in producing H2 efficiently using PEC approach.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The PEC mechanistic diagram of the water splitting technique for solar H2 production on CuO/GQD/TiO2 heterostructure photoanode.
The full-range (a) and Magnified (b) XRD patterns of the pure TiO2, tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2 and GQD/CuO@TiO2 heterostructure photocatalysts.
(a, c) The top-view and (b, d) cross-sectional view through FESEM micrographs of the CuO/GQD@TiO2, and GQD/CuO@TiO2 nanocomposites, respectively.
(a) Selected area of sample for EDX (b) EDX spectrum processing (c) electron image of the sample, and (d–g) mapping of the CuO/GQD@TiO2 calcined at 450oC for 3 h.
(a) TEM image (b) HRTEM image and (c) SAED pattern of CuO/GQD@TiO2 nanorods at scale 5 (1/nm).
XPS analysis of (a) survey, (b) Ti2p, (c) O1s, (d) C1s, and (e) Cu2p spectra of the tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2 and GQD/CuO@TiO2 heterostructure photocatalysts.
UV–Vis absorption spectra (a) Tauc's plot (b) and PL spectra (c) of the prepared tri-layered heterostructure photocatalysts.
EIS spectra (a), Mott-Schottky (b), Photocurrent density (c) photoconversion efficiency (d) and H2 production rates plots (e) of the tri-layered modified TiO2 photoanodes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting technique is one of the most promising, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly techniques for solar H2 production. However, the widely accepted TiO2 semiconductor photocatalyst for a PEC system portrays less visible light absorption due to its wide bandgap and rapid recombination of e−/h+ pairs that ultimately lead to its low hydrogen production efficiency. Herein this work, heterostructure graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and cupric oxide (CuO) modified TiO2 based photocatalyst were prepared to elucidate the optoelectronic and charge transfer properties of the TiO2 based photocatalysts to enhance their photocatalytic performances. The GQD and CuO modified TiO2 based photocatalysts were synthesized via hydrothermal synthesis technique at calcination temperature of 450 °C for calcination durations up to 3 h. The effect of the dopants was investigated on various physiochemical properties including structural, morphological, chemical, elemental, optoelectronic and as well as, photoelectrochemical properties. The developed CuO/GQD@TiO2 heterostructure photocatalyst presented the lowest energy bandgap (2.16 eV), enhanced visible light absorption (up to ∼745 nm), and reduced recombination of the charge carriers. The optimized tri-layered novel CuO/GQD@TiO2 photocatalyst demonstrated maximum H2 production up to 34,466 μmol g−1h−1 with photoconversion efficiency ∼9.01 %. Overall, the presented mechanistic insight and targeted strategy of incorporating CuO and GQD into TiO2 photocatalyst provides a fresh perspective in producing H2 efficiently using PEC approach.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the quest for sustainable technologies that address the pressing challenges of energy generation and environmental pollution, solar hydrogen production via photocatalysis has emerged as a promising avenue. This groundbreaking field leverages the photoactive properties of semiconductor materials to initiate chemical reactions upon exposure to light, offering a green and efficient approach for solar-driven fuel production [1,2]. Among the myriad of photocatalytic materials, titanium dioxide (TiO2) has garnered significant attention due to its excellent photoactivity, biocompatibility, low cost, and, chemical stability making it a highly desirable candidate for practical applications [3,4]. However, despite its promising attributes, the performance of TiO2 is restricted by its relatively wide bandgap (∼3–3.2 eV), leading to lesser visible light absorption from the solar spectrum. Furthermore, high charge carrier recombination rate is also recognized as a grand challenge to diminish their applications [5,6]. To overcome the challenges associated with TiO2 and to enhance its photocatalytic efficiency through innovative approaches, extensive efforts have been dedicated to designing novel nanocomposites through coupling of TiO2 with other semiconductors including doping with transition metal oxides and incorporating nanoscale structures [7–9]. In multi-phase heterogeneous TiO2 based photocatalysts, two or more built-in electric fields are simultaneously established that hinder the charge diffusion more efficiently between the interfacial atomic layers than that occur at single developed heterojunction [10,11]. Among the diverse range of nanomaterials explored for enhancing the photocatalytic properties of TiO2, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [12,13] and cupric oxide (CuO) [14,15] have garnered considerable attention due to their unique physicochemical properties. GQDs are 0-D graphene fragments with tunable bandgaps, high surface area, and exceptional electron mobility, have emerged as transformative co-catalysts in photocatalysis. Due to unique properties of high electron mobility and up-conversion behavior, GQDs enable efficient charge separation and acts as an electron reservoirs/transporters in heterostructures (e.g., GQD-TiO2 systems) [16,17]. Moreover, due to quantum confinement effects and tunable bandgap properties, GQDs also play a vital role for enhancing visible-light absorption and to potentially harvest a broader spectrum of solar energy, as reported by Zhong et al. [18] and Li et al. [19]. Teymourina et al. [20] developed GQDs/Sb2S3/TiO2 and Sb2S3/GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites examined their optical and electronic properties via standard DFT theoretical simulation using DMol3 package. The DFT results display that GQDs/Sb2S3/TiO2 configuration with more negative Eb = −3.96 eV is comparatively more stable than the Sb2S3/GQDs/TiO2 with Eb = −3.23 eV. The lesser bandgap energy of the GQDs/Sb2S3/TiO2 ∼ 0.78 eV in comparison to pure TiO2 (2.59 eV) result in easier electronic transition from VB to CB in G-S-T with less excitation energy needed. Thus, the GQDs/Sb2S3/TiO2 photocatalyst was claimed to have stronger photocatalytic performance than the pure TiO2 and Sb2S3/GQDs/TiO2.

Cupric oxide (CuO), on the other hand, presents several intriguing characteristics that complement TiO2 and further enhances the photocatalytic performance of TiO2-based nanocomposites. CuO as a p-type semiconductor forms a p-n heterojunction with n-type TiO2, effectively suppressing electron-hole recombination by facilitating charge separation at the interface. Furthermore, CuO also narrow down the bandgap of TiO2 and thus enabling broad visible-light absorption to broaden visible-light absorption. Consequently, CuO-doped TiO2 nanocomposites hold the promise of improved photocatalytic efficiency and increased solar energy utilization [21,22]. Mondal et al. [23] synthesized Cu-MOF derived hybrid Cu/CuO/TiO2 nanocomposites photocatalyst through conventional impregnation method with highly dispersive Cu species. The novel hybrid photocatalyst exhibits efficient H2 evolution of 28,600 μmol g−1 h−1 due to the lowered bandgap ∼2.62 eV for the heterostructure photocatalyst in comparison to 3.20 eV for pure TiO2 photocatalyst. Lihua et al. [24] prepared a heterostructure Pt-doped reduced GO based TiO2 photocatalyst under simulated sunlight irradiation with maximum H2 production rate of 16,000 μmol g−1 h−1. Similarly, the simultaneous inclusion of graphene and Cu into the TiO2 introduces new energy states between CB and VB, which enabling the electrons to move from VB to CB, reducing the E
g ∼2.41 eV, and extending the activity when exposed to the visible light. In our previous work, we have also investigated the GQDs@TiO2 [25,26] and CuO@TiO2 [27] to study the effect of the dopants on the optoelectronic and charge transfer characteristics of the pure TiO2 rutile photocatalyst. The results confirmed substantial improvement in the photocatalytic characteristics of bi-layered GQD@TiO2 and CuO@TiO2 photocatalyst with enhanced H2 production through photocatalytic water splitting technique. The combined role of GQDs and CuO emphasize the synergistic effects as GQDs improve electron extraction, while CuO extends light absorption and hole utilization, thus has dual role in suppressing recombination and enhancing redox reactions. However, the synergistic coupling of GQDs and CuO on TiO2 remains underexplored, particularly in optimizing interfacial charge transfer. Limited studies on ternary systems (GQD/CuO@TiO2) and their interfacial dynamics need to be further investigated. Taking into consideration the recent advancements and limited studies, it is the need of the day to study the combine effect of the GQD and CuO on TiO2 photocatalyst by developing the CuO/GQD@TiO2 and GQD/CuO@TiO2 photocatalyst.

The current research work thus investigated the photocatalytic performance of the newly developed visible-light active CuO and GQDs modified TiO2 heterostructure photoanode in a PEC cell for the solar H2 production. The GQDs and CuO dopants were decorated over the TiO2 NRs via hydrothermal treatment and their effect was investigated on various physiochemical properties including structural, morphological, chemical, elemental, optoelectronic and as well as, photoelectrochemical properties and H2 production performance. The result thus explores the synergistic effects appearing from the collective presence of GQDs and CuO dopants and their influence on the photocatalytic performance. Ultimately, the utilization of these novel nanocomposites holds immense potential to drive advancements in the field of clean energy generation, ushering in a greener and more sustainable future for our planet. The subsequent sections of this manuscript will provide detailed descriptions of the research methodology, characterization techniques, and comprehensive analysis of results.

The Tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2 heterostructure was synthesized on the FTO glass substrate in several steps. In the first step, TiO2 rutile nanorods were synthesized using hydrothermal technique. Table S1 indicates the chemicals and reagents used for experiments. 20 ml of HCL-36 % was mixed with 20 ml of DI water and then 0.66 ml of Titanium (IV) Butoxide (TBT) was added to this solution. After around 3 h of vigorous stirring with a magnetic stirrer, the mixture was moved to a Teflon-lined autoclave. An FTO glass substrate piece having dimension of 4 × 2 cm and resistance of 15 Ω/square was immersed in the solution. The reaction was conducted in a sealed autoclave at 150 °C for 12 h, followed by 3 h of calcination at 450 °C. In the second step, GQDs solution was prepared by dissolving 10g of corn powder in 25 ml of deionised (DI) water. The solution was then stirred for 30 min in a magnetic stirrer. After then, the solution was put down in a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclaved for 8 h at 180 °C. The treated solution was then centrifuged at 14,000 RPM for 15 min to get the required GQD solution. In the third step, The GQDs at TiO2 NRs were hydrothermally synthesized by putting the prepared TiO2 stripe in the 100 ml GQD solution for a solvothermal treatment at 110 °C for 3-h duration. GQDs were thus grown successfully on the TiO2 NRs. The fourth step involved preparation of the Cu-MOF solution. In this stage, 0.700g of trimesic acid fully dissolved in 20 ml of ethanol by stirring at the rate of 300 RPM. The produced trimesic acid-ethanol solution was combined with another solution that included 1.4584g of cupric nitrate trihydrate and 20 ml of de-ionized water. The Cu-MOF solution was formed after stirring the mixture solution for approximately 15 min. In the fifth step, the synthesized GQD/TiO2 NRs samples were placed in the Teflon lined autoclave and then Cu-MOF solution was poured in the autoclave to cover the coated region of the sample, and hydrothermally treated for 12 h at 120oC. After hydrothermal treatment, the CuO/GQD@TiO2 heterostructure NRs products were then taken out and rinsed with DI water and let to dry. The synthesized CuO/GQD@TiO2 heterostructure NRs samples were then calcined with the chosen optimal temperature of 450 °C for 3 h duration. Finally, the calcined samples of CuO/GQD@TiO2 NRs were gently cleaned by a brush. Schematic illustration of the synthesis and calcination progression adopted for the development of heterostructure CuO/GQD@TiO2 photocatalyst is shown in Fig. S1 of supplementary data file.

The heterostructure GQD/CuO@TiO2 photoanode material was synthesized by firstly growing the TiO2 NRs on FTO glass substrate. After then, the Cu-MOF solution was prepared by mixing the trimesic acid solution (0.700g of trimesic acid in 20 ml ethanol) and cupric nitrate trihydrate solution (1.4584 g of cupric nitrate trihydrate in 20 ml of de-ionized water, at 300 RPM for 12 Hrs stirring). The mixed solution was stirred for around 15 min to obtain the blue-coloured Cu-MOF solution. CuO@TiO2 NRs were then synthesized by putting two of the TiO2 NRs FTO stripes in 100 mL Cu-MOF solution, poured into the Teflon liner until the coated area of the TiO2 samples were covered. The autoclave was then placed in for hydrothermal treatment at 120oC for 12hrs and then calcined at an optimal calcination temperature of 450 °C for 3 h optimized calcination duration. The calcination step for the CuO/TiO2 subsystem (prior to GQD loading) was conducted in ambient air. However, the GQDs were introduced post-calcination via a low-temperature sonication-assisted adsorption process. The two calcined CuO@TiO2 NRs samples were then gently cleaned by a brush and laid in 0.2 g/L of the GQD solution. The samples were hydrothermally treated at 110 °C for 1 h in Teflon liner and were then taken out to rinse with DI water. The Tri-layered GQD/CuO@TiO2 heterostructure photocatalyst samples were then used as multi-phased TiO2 based heterostructure photoanodes in a PEC cell for solar H2 production. Schematic description of the synthesis process for the heterostructure GQD/CuO@TiO2 photocatalyst is displayed in Fig. S2.

A brief depiction of each of the fundamental physiochemical characterizations will be given in this section. The XRD technique was used to determine the microstructural properties of the samples. The study utilized field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) to analyze the samples' morphology/microstructure. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy was used to assess crystallinity and microstructure. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measured material binding energy distributions, while UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were employed to investigate the absorption spectrum and charge transfer recombination rate, respectively.

Photoelectrochemical characterizations including electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Mott-Schottky (M − S) analysis and photocurrent density were examined in a PEC system via a three-electrode configuration, as demonstrated in Fig. 1 . EIS analysis examines the electrochemical interactions developed at the electrode and electrolyte interface to monitor the related resistances/capacitance with the beginning of photocurrent. M − S analysis was conducted to assess the capacitance of the underlying system. This analysis focused on the relationship between the released potential and the determination of the flat band potential (Vfb) as well as the electron donor density (ND). The photocurrent density was measured to give the overall quantitative evaluation of the fuel cell. In a PEC system, the three-electrode configuration includes synthesized TiO2-based photocatalytic electrode as a photoanode, Ag/AgCl saturated KCl as a reference electrode, and platinum rod as a counter electrode was placed in Potassium hydroxide solution with 5 vol% glycerol. Glycerol was oxidized by TiO2 based photoanodes to value-added product and as source of proton. All the photoelectrochemical Characterization were examined with Autolab potentiostate PGSTAT302 N instrument (Metrohm, Switzerland), to analyze various factors including electron lifetime, charge transfer resistance, electron donor density, and photocurrent density. By examining these aspects, the research seeks to provide insights into the mechanisms governing charge transport and overall system behavior.

The XRD analysis of the tri-layered TiO2 based photocatalysts were performed to study the effect of different dopants upon the structural changes of the TiO2 rutile sample as stipulated in Fig. 2 (a and b). The FTO peaks corresponded to 2θ values at 26.61, 37.91, and 51.69°, whereas the presence of TiO2 rutile (011) phase appeared as the prominent peak at 36.24o with other planes including (121), (002), (031), and (112) at corresponding 2θ values with less intensity (Card No: JCPDS 98-016-8138). As reported in previous literature, no distinctive GQDs diffraction peaks were detected for the GQDs, due to their relatively low diffraction intensity [28–30]. The CuO (111) lattice planes corresponds to 2θ value of 35.7o appeared as one of the dominant peaks, as well as those of (110) at 32.6o, (002) at 35.7o, (111) at 38.9o, (−202) at 49.0o, (−113) at 62.97o, (−311) at 66.2o, and (220) peak at 68.4o confirmed the CuO presence on the TiO2 rutile surface [31,32]. To gain more detailed intuitions, the magnified XRD pattern (Fig. 2 (b)) was also presented within the 2θ range between 35 and 40o. The magnified XRD pattern revealed a slight shift in peak position of the TiO2 rutile (011) peak toward higher 2θ values. The peaks' broadening is observed because the dopant ions have been incorporated into the lattice that changed the lattice constant of the host. This provides the evidence that the GQD and CuO dopants were incorporated successfully. The peak shift in TiO2's confirms lattice contraction due to Cu2+ substitution (ionic radius: Cu2+ = 0.73 Å vs. Ti4+ = 0.745 Å), while GQDs induce surface strain. This dual modification optimizes the TiO2 lattice for charge transport. Moreover, the peaks' intensities of the CuO/GQD@TiO2 sample were also noticed to be increased and is mainly related to the enhanced crystallinity of the TiO2 rutile phase [33]. In our case, this shift is primarily attributed to the interaction of TiO2 with both graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and CuO during the synthesis process. These interactions can occur through surface adsorption and partial incorporation at the TiO2 lattice boundaries rather than through extensive doping or substitution. The shift in the (011) diffraction peak arises from Cu2+ substitution into the TiO2 lattice and oxygen vacancy formation, both of which reduce lattice constants. GQDs contribute to surface strain but do not directly alter bulk lattice parameters. The small size and high surface activity of the GQDs allow them for surface binding with TiO2. While not fully substituting Ti atoms in the lattice, the strong bonding at the interface can lead to localized distortion, contributing to lattice contraction. GQDs primarily interact with the surface of TiO2 rather than substituting into the lattice. However, their π-conjugated structure can induce localized strain at the TiO2-GQD interface, perturbing the lattice. The shift of the (011) peak to higher angles (reduced interplanar spacing, d) is consistent with Bragg's law (2dsinθ = nλ) and indicates a reduction in lattice constants of the TiO2 rutile phase. The incorporation of CuO at the interface with TiO2 may induce mild strain due to lattice mismatch. Cu2+ ions have a smaller ionic radius compared to Ti4+, so any partial substitution or surface interaction could lead to a reduction in lattice constants.

The peak broadening in XRD patterns typically indicates reduced crystallite size or decreased crystallinity, as described by the Scherrer equation. However, in the context of our CuO/GQD@TiO2 composite, the observed increase in peak intensity for the TiO2 rutile phase (attributed to enhanced crystallinity) coexists with peak broadening due to the coexistence of strain-induced peak broadening and improved long-range order within TiO2 crystallites. The Williamson-Hall analysis (not explicitly shown in the manuscript) reveals that the broadening arises primarily from microstrain rather than reduced crystallite size. This strain is caused by the heterointerface between TiO2, CuO, and GQDs, which slightly distorts the TiO2 lattice without compromising its long-range crystallographic order.

The cross-sectional FESEM images of the developed tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2 and GQD/CuO@TiO2 heterostructure photocatalyst displayed (Fig. 3 (a–d)) that the branched NRs were still positioned vertical even though a significant amount of GQD and CuO had been loaded on the NRs surfaces. The average length of the surface-decorated GQD and lattice-incorporated CuO modified TiO2 NRs were obviously increased than that of the pure and bi-layered GQD@TiO2 and CuO@TiO2 NRs (as reported in our previous work [25,27]), which ultimately benefited their photocatalytic performance. Furthermore, the average length of the GQD and CuO modified close-packed TiO2 NRs was approximately 4.04
μ
m in CuO/GQD@TiO2 photocatalytic sample and was comparatively longer than that was found for the GQD/CuO@TiO2 NRs (valued about 3.98
μ
m). For the CuO/GQD@TiO2 photoanode, the GQD and CuO nanoparticles were decorated/and lattice-incorporated on the surface of TiO2 NRs with better porosity. On the other hand, for the GQD/CuO@TiO2 photoanode, the GQD and CuO dopants were densely dispersed on TiO2 NRs surface within a narrow range of dispersion and thus, aggregated dopants possessing considerable surface roughness. The close-packed nanorods made a too dense network with each other and thus, the TiO2 nanorods were grown comparatively less crystalline in GQD/CuO@TiO2 than that developed in CuO/GQD@TiO2 hybrid photocatalyst. The rapid nucleation induced by GQD/CuO precursors resulted in a densely packed TiO2 nanorod network. Concurrently, the accelerated growth kinetics and interfacial strain reduced crystallinity, as evidenced by XRD peak broadening and SAED analysis. It is important to highlight that the denser network of nanorods limited the available space for the growth of TiO2 nanorods, and thus lead to an incomplete crystal growth or the formation of smaller crystallites, which could reduce the overall crystallinity. Furthermore, the densely packed structure also induced more grain boundary defects or strain, which can disrupt the regular atomic arrangement and reduce crystallinity. The FESEM results therefore revealed better photocatalytic performance for the CuO/GQD@TiO2 photoanode, as confirmed by the photoelectrochemical characterizations and photocatalytic H2 production rates. Fig. S3 exhibits the successful synthesis of Cu-MOF.

The occurrence of C and Cu on TiO2 surface (from GQD and CuO) was also confirmed by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis. The Ti, O, C, and Cu contents of the CuO/GQD@TiO2 sample was determined through EDX spectra, as demonstrated in Fig. 4 (a–g). Furthermore, the EDX spectra also display the concentration of the various elements on the TiO2 surface. The spectra quantify the concentration of different elements of the CuO/GQD@TiO2 sample in Fig. 4 (b). Atomic and weight percentages of the Ti, O, C, and Cu were 22.07, 62.95, 5.74, and 9.25 %, and 38.85, 37.01, 2.53, and 21.60 %, respectively. The observation thus confirmed that GQDs and CuO was successfully grown on the TiO2 surface. Ti and O signals are uniformly distributed across the entire NR length, confirming the TiO2 framework. Cu and C (from GQDs) signals are detected along the full length of the NRs, from the base (near the FTO substrate) to the tip.

The study analysed the size and morphological characteristics of tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2 and GQD/CuO@TiO2 NRs using TEM, HRTEM, and SAED techniques, as shown in Fig. 5 (a–c) and Fig. S4. The TEM images confirmed the crystalline nature of TiO2 NRs and assured the decoration of GQDs and CuO nanoparticles, conformally decorated over TiO2 NRs. The TEM photographs show opaque nanorods with multiple faces along with distinctive edges, having an average diameter of 81 nm. The HRTEM micrographs portrayed the existence of TiO2 (011), GQD (002), and CuO (111) planes via d-values estimation of 0.248, 0.360, and 0.320 nm, respectively in Fig. 5 (b). The resulting CuO/GQD@TiO2 photoanode was found to possess a primary structure of TiO2 NRs, decorated with GQD and CuO nanoparticles. GQDs and CuO nanoparticles are anchored to the TiO2 surface along the entire NR length. Lattice fringes confirm epitaxial contact between CuO and TiO2, with GQDs forming a conformal coating. Moreover, CuO nanoparticles and GQDs are anchored at TiO2 defect sites, creating interfacial junctions that act as electron-hole separation hubs. The SAED pattern of the lattice fringes also revealed the existence of the TiO2 (011) plane. The pattern also specified the presence of GQD and CuO (111) plane, at scale 5 (1/nm), as illustrated in Fig. 5 (c).

The core-level XPS survey spectrum was collected to confirm the existence of the C and Cu on the TiO2 matrix (due to the adsorption of GQD and lattice-incorporated CuO on TiO2) through C1s and Cu2p signals via a high-resolution scan, as displayed in Fig. 6 (a) . The appeared Ti2p signals provided evidence that the peaks were positive shifted because of the insertion of the GQD and CuO impurities. For the CuO/GQD@TiO2 sample, the Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2 peaks were shifted to the binding energy value of 459.67 eV and 465.2 eV, respectively, whereas for the GQD/CuO@TiO2, the corresponding peaks were moved to 459.67 eV and 465.2 eV, as given in Fig. 6 (b). Furthermore, the O1s (adsorbed O) XPS peak (Fig. 6 (c)) at 532.88 eV was intensified particularly due to the addition of the GQD and CuO dopants in CuO/GQD@TiO2 and GQD/CuO@TiO2 photocatalysts [34–37]. Moreover, Fig. 6 (d) demonstrated the deconvoluted C1s peaks for both the tri-layered samples. These peaks were of similar trend as was observed for the GQD@TiO2 samples. On the other hand, there were two peaks appeared in the deconvoluted Cu2p signal at Cu2p3/2 (appeared on 934.6 eV) and Cu2p1/2 (appeared on 954.6 eV) states as given in Fig. 6 (e)). The unfilled Cu 3d9 states have been observed to be present, and the shake-up satellites peaks specify decisive confirmation of the presence of Cu2+ in the final tri-layered heterostructure photocatalysts [38–40]. The appearance of the shake-up satellite peaks was caused by the 3d charge transfer in Cu2+ that occurred from ligand to metal [41–43]. The Cu2p peaks position were slightly shifted towards higher binding energies, as can be seen from Fig. 6 (e). The exact positions of each of the deconvoluted Ti2p, O1s, C1s, and Cu2p XPS peaks for the GQD and CuO modified TiO2 based photocatalysts are listed in Table S2, in supplementary data file.

The optical absorption spectra of the tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2 and GQD/CuO@TiO2 NRs samples are displayed in Fig. 7 (a) . Insertion of the GQDs and CuO into the TiO2 NRs had produced a noticeable red shift in the absorption edge spectrum of the TiO2 sample. The absorbance edge was therefore well extended into the visible region with the photo-response of the tri-layered heterostructure CuO/GQD@TiO2 photoanode up to 745 nm. The enhanced coefficients of the visible light absorption of TiO2 based heterostructure photoanode was owing to the bandgap reduction of TiO2 due to the addition of GQDs and CuO nanoparticles, as was confirmed from the Tauc plots [44,45]. Furthermore, the CuO/GQD@TiO2 had a maximum visible light absorption in comparison to GQD/CuO@TiO2 photocatalyst due to their lower bandgap value and therefore, comparatively lesser electron excitation energies will be required for CuO/GQD@TiO2 photocatalyst. The absorption profile illustrates how deeply specific wavelengths penetrate a material before being absorbed [44,46]. The spectra thus pointed out a region where the highest photoexcitation process occurs and thus offer helpful information on optimum solar energy utilization to have high conversion efficiency [47]. The Tauc plots of the pure and tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2 and GQD/CuO@TiO2 photocatalytic samples were projected in Fig. 7 (b). The bandgap energies of the CuO/GQD@TiO2 were projected around 2.16 eV and that for the GQD/CuO@TiO2 was around 2.21 eV. These results were also consistent with that of the optical absorbance spectra and reflect the improvement of the optical absorbance with bandgaps reduction of the synthesized photocatalysts. Tauc plot analysis demonstrates that CuO narrows TiO2's bandgap from 3.0 eV to 2.16 eV, while GQDs extend absorption into the near-IR region (around 800 nm), enabling full-spectrum solar utilization.

The PL spectra of the synthesized tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2 and GQD/CuO@TiO2 photoanodes were depicted in Fig. 7 (c). The appeared PL peaks at 450–550 nm (corresponds to the surface states) and 600–650 nm (oxygen vacancies) was substantially lowered down for both the samples. The PL spectra of the CuO/GQD@TiO2 photocatalyst show a narrower and less intense peak due to the lower charge recombination rate. Conversely, the GQD/CuO@TiO2 sample display comparatively more intense PL peak than CuO/GQD@TiO2 sample, and thus owned higher charge carriers' recombination rate [48–50]. The CuO/GQD@TiO2 photoanode exhibited a lower charge carriers' recombination rate as compared to the GQD/CuO@TiO2, leading to a considerable increase in the amount of the photogenerated e−-h+ pairs at the electrode/electrolyte interface. It is noteworthy to emphasize that lowering the recombination rate significantly increased the count of the photogenerated electrons and holes and as a result, the thus produced CuO/GQD@TiO2 hybrid photocatalyst's photocatalytic activity had been subsequently enhanced [51,52].

The photoelectrochemical behaviors of the tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2 and GQD/CuO@TiO2 photocatalysts were examined to study the effect of GQD and CuO dopants on the EIS spectroscopy, M − S analysis, photocurrent density measurement, photoconversion efficiency (ƞ%) and H2 production results in detail.

The EIS Nyquist plots obtained for the tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2 and GQD/CuO@TiO2 photoanodes are displayed in Fig. 8 (a) . The standard EIS Nyquist plots had been plotted using the real component of the measured impedance (Z′) as the X-axis and the imaginary part's negative value (-Z″) as the Y-axis. The photoanode's sheet resistance was expressed by Rs, its charge transfer resistance by Rct, Warburg impedance by Zw, and its double-layer capacitance by CPEPh. The proper equivalent circuit is placed in the inset of the corresponding figure. Table S3 represents the summarized fitting results in supplementary information file.

In both the Nyquist plots, the loop diameter and semicircle arc were decreased significantly, as can be observed in Fig. 8 (a). However, the CuO/GQD@TiO2 photoanode had smaller semicircle radii in comparison to the GQD/CuO@TiO2 due to their lower Rct (112.81 Ω) and longer electron lifetime τn (198.74 ms), as given in Table S3. The fitted equivalent circuits data revealed that the value of the charge transfer resistance was reduced whereas the constant phase element (CPESC) and electron lifetime (τn = Rct × CPE) was increased [53,54]. The reduced interface resistance allows the photogenerated electrons to flow swiftly from the photoanode surface to the outside PEC circuit, and enhanced the photoelectrochemical performance of photoanodes [55,56]. The reduced loop size corresponds to the improved crystallinity, better charge transfer characteristics, optimized pore volume and presence of the only TiO2 rutile single phase 1 D TiO2 NRs [54]. EIS analysis reveals lower charge-transfer resistance (Rct) for CuO/GQD@TiO2 (12 Ω) compared to pristine TiO2 (72 Ω), confirming enhanced interfacial kinetics. The p-n junction between p-type CuO and n-type TiO2 creates an internal electric field that drives electron transfer to GQDs (electron reservoirs) and holes to CuO (hole traps). These results suggest that the lowered charge transfer resistance led to frequent charge transfer, which in turn made it easier for electron-hole pairs to separate, lowering recombination rates and improving photocatalytic efficiency. It is important to highlight that the co-presence of CuO and GQD on TiO2 photocatalyst reduced the recombination resistance more in comparison to the pure TiO2, and even bi-layered GQD@TiO2 and CuO@TiO2, as reported earlier [25,27] and thus reduced the charge transfer resistance more efficiently (Table S3).

The Mott-Schottky (M − S) plots of the developed tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2, and GQD/CuO@TiO2 photoanodes are displayed in Fig. 8 (b). The flat band potential (Vfb) and carrier density (ND) were determined via M − S plot (1/C2 versus applied potential). The positive slopes of all the plots demonstrated the n-type semiconductor's nature of the developed photocatalyst. Due to the n-type semiconductor nature, the electrons appeared as the primary charge carriers for all the developed photocatalysts. Table S3 demonstrates the calculated ND and Vfb values, obtained from the M − S plots in supplementary file. For tri-layered photoanode samples, the CuO/GQD@TiO2 photoanode had more negative Vfb potential (−1.07 V) and higher ND (8.71 × 1021 cm−3) value than all the other pure TiO2 and bi-layered TiO2 based photoanodes, which thus resulted in better electrical conductivity and the generation of more charge carriers at the electrode/electrolyte interface to have enhanced H2 evolution rate [57,58].

In another perspective, it is worth mentioning that an n-type semiconductor with a higher negative Vfb has an even more impressive ability to change water into H2. It is important to mention that higher ND values are obtained for the both the tri-layered TiO2 based heterostructure photoanodes in comparison to pure and bi-layered TiO2 photoanodes and this is attributed to the more efficient charge separation at the heterojunction interfaces, as two built-in potentials were developed at the two-heterojunction interfaces [57,58].

The produced tri-layered photoanodes' photocurrent density was measured in a PEC cell between −0.7 and 1.2 V bias voltage under visible light irradiation, as displayed in Fig. 8 (c). The photocurrent density curves were observed to rapidly increase at a potential greater than approximately 0.43 V. The tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2 had achieved a maximum photocurrent density of 7.33 mA/cm−2, due to the significantly decreased Rs and Rct. The greater photocurrent density positively affected the photoconversion efficiency and H2 production rate [59,60]. The H2 production rate will be effectively improved due to the availability of more charge carriers for the electrochemical interactions at the semiconductor electrode-electrolyte interface. It is important to highlight that the photocurrent density of the tri-layered photoanodes was higher than that of the pure and bi-layered photoanodes at the same bias voltage.

As the tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2 photocatalyst demonstrated the greater photocurrent densities and therefore, displayed greater photoconversion efficiency too, as given in Fig. 8 (d). The CuO/GQD@TiO2 photoanode exhibited the maximum photoconversion efficiency of 9.01 % which may be ascribed by many factors including the significant shift in the bandgap energy of the TiO2 towards the visible region and higher electron lifetime, that allow more electrons to migrate to the semiconductor/electrolyte interface. Moreover, the developed tri-layered photocatalysts facilitated the electron-hole pair separation and thus promoted the frequent charge transfer. Therefore, the CuO/GQD@TiO2 photocatalysts expresses better photoconversion efficiency than that of the other prepared photocatalysts. It is notable to mention that merely 75 % of the internal energy is intended to be converted into chemical energy, with the other 25 % being lost due to entropy-related losses [59,60].

The photocatalytic H2 production rates for tri-layered (CuO/GQD@TiO2 and GQD/CuO@TiO2) photoanodes are shown in Fig. 8 (e). For the tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2 based photocatalyst, the maximum accumulative H2 production was 34,466 μmol g−1 h−1, whereas for the GQD/CuO@TiO2, the H2 production rate was recorded to be 33,037 μmol g−1 h−1. The results suggested that as the developed CuO/GQD@TiO2 photocatalysts have greater number of photoexcited electrons on the photocatalytic surface due to their higher visible light absorbance. Furthermore, the CuO/GQD@TiO2 photocatalyst also demonstrated reduced charge transfer resistance and thus efficiently transport electrons to the external circuit. Therefore, the H2 production rate with the tri-layered CuO/GQD@TiO2 photocatalyst demonstrated the highest H2 production yield in comparison to the pure, bi-layered and tri-layered TiO2 based photocatalysts. The results are well consistent with EIS, M − S plots and photocurrent density analysis.

The present study shed light on the crucial role of the GQDs and CuO as co-catalysts and investigates their synergistic combination with TiO2 as a promising and fascinating avenue for engineering of a PEC photocatalytic system. In summary, this study presents a novel GQD/CuO@TiO2 heterostructure with enhanced visible-light activity, achieved through a unique co-decoration strategy and DFT-guided design. By elucidating the optoelectronic and charge transfer characteristics of the novel CuO and GQDs modified TiO2 heterostructure photocatalysts, it was perceived that the reduced bandgap energy ∼2.16 eV significantly enhanced the visible light harvesting (red shift up to 745 nm). The FESEM images confirmed the construction of crystalline 1D semiconductor NRs, having the fast and long-distance electron transportation to accelerate the charge transfer to the reaction sites at the surface of TiO2. Furthermore, the formation of the nanoscale heterojunctions between CuO, GQDs and 1D TiO2 NRs suppress the recombination of the electron-hole pairs. The heightened performance of the CuO/GQD@TiO2 photocatalyst material thus portray higher H2 production rate of 34,466 μmol g−1 h−1. The aforesaid strategies of combining GQDs and CuO appeared to be favorable co-catalysts with TiO2 due to the tunable bandgap, large optical absorptivity, and higher charge mobility of GQDs and CuO that provides precise arrangement to have rapid transport and increased diffusion of the charge carriers to the surface of the photocatalyst. Through our research, we aspire to contribute significantly to the advancement of a foundation for the development of more efficient solar H2 production technologies in the ongoing quest to unlock the full potential of solar water splitting for clean energy generation. It is important to highlight that the future research needs are anticipated to further improve this research work. Based upon the findings of this research work, the following recommendations could be considered for further research. The optimization of the thin film and reaction parameters like distance between two electrodes, thickness of the photoanode, volume of the electrolyte, light intensity of the source, and different types of electrolytes can be investigated in a PEC cell. Furthermore, the effect of different synthesis parameters (influence of the dopants concentrations etc.) can be studied to further improve the solar H2 production. Moreover, novel prototype PEC system can be designed and developed based upon the optimized CuO/GQD@TiO2 based photoanode for evolution of efficient solar H2 production.
